compositions.name: Botpress
compositions.license: MIT (legacy v12 OSS); proprietary cloud platform
compositions.intent: Visual + code AI agent platform (Studio drag-and-drop, ADK TypeScript library, Desk for human handoff, Webchat, Hub integrations) for building LLM-powered chatbots with autonomous nodes, knowledge bases, and event triggers.
compositions.description: Botpress is a complete AI agent platform combining a visual Studio (drag-and-drop), an ADK (TypeScript library for code-first agents), Desk (customer support workspace for human-AI teams), Webchat, and a Hub of integrations. Agent behaviour is built from Workflows containing Nodes, including Autonomous Nodes that delegate decisions to an LLM with tools. Eight built-in Agents (Summary, Personality, Policy, Translator, Knowledge, Vision, Analytics, HITL) run in a fixed sequence each turn. The legacy v12 OSS engine is MIT-licensed; the current cloud Studio/Desk product is proprietary.
primary_use_cases: customer-support chatbots with human handoff to live agents via Desk or HITL plugin
visual no-code agent design with LLM-backed Autonomous Nodes
code-first agents built with the Botpress ADK (TypeScript)
multi-channel conversational agents across Webchat, Slack, WhatsApp, Teams, and other Hub integrations
knowledge-base-grounded Q&A agents over uploaded documents and websites
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Per-turn execution runs eight built-in Agents in a fixed order (Summary, Personality, Policy, Translator, Knowledge, Vision, Router, Analytics, HITL). The bot's logic itself is a graph of Workflows of Nodes; a standard Node executes its Cards top to bottom, while an Autonomous Node hands control to an LLM that decides which tools to call and which transition to take. Triggers (e.g. Conversation Started, Custom Triggers from Webchat or integrations) enter Workflows at well-defined entry points.

key_concepts.name: Autonomous Node
key_concepts.summary: LLM-driven node that picks tools, writes replies, and chooses transitions from context.

key_concepts.name: Agents (Summary/Personality/Policy/Translator/Knowledge/Vision/Analytics/HITL)
key_concepts.summary: Eight built-in Agents that extend a bot's capabilities; they execute in a fixed sequence each turn.

key_concepts.name: Knowledge Base
key_concepts.summary: Documents, websites, and files the bot can query for grounded answers.

key_concepts.name: Triggers
key_concepts.summary: Nodes that only execute when a named event fires (conversation start, custom Webchat events, integration events).

key_concepts.name: Desk / HITL plugin
key_concepts.summary: Escalates conversations from the bot to a human agent in Botpress Desk or a third-party platform.

key_concepts.name: Conversation variables
key_concepts.summary: Per-conversation scoped state cleared when the conversation ends.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User message] --> trigger{Trigger?}
  trigger -->|Conversation Started / Custom| entry[Workflow entry]
  entry --> agents[Built-in Agents pipeline]
  agents --> summary[Summary] --> personality[Personality] --> policy[Policy] --> translator[Translator]
  translator --> knowledge[Knowledge KB lookup]
  knowledge --> node{Node type}
  node -->|Standard| cards[Execute Cards sequentially]
  node -->|Autonomous| llm[LLM picks tools and transitions]
  llm --> tools[Tools / integrations]
  tools --> respond[Send messages]
  cards --> respond
  respond --> hitl{Escalate?}
  hitl -->|yes| desk[Desk / HITL plugin to human agent]
  hitl -->|no| done[End turn]
  desk --> done

members.note: Built-in Agents extend a bot's capabilities, but they are a fixed catalogue (Summary, Personality, Policy, Translator, Knowledge, Vision, Analytics, HITL) running in a fixed order, not user-authored skills.

evidence.title: Botpress agents introduction
evidence.quote: Agents extend your bot's capabilities beyond its basic functions.

members.note: Knowledge Bases are a documented first-class resource for grounding bot answers in documents/websites/files; the Knowledge Agent retrieves from them.

evidence.title: Botpress docs index — Knowledge bases
evidence.quote: Give your agent access to documents, websites, and files.

evidence.title: Botpress llms.txt — knowledge bases
evidence.quote: Knowledge Bases: Resource system enabling bots to access documents, websites, and files for information retrieval.

members.note: Desk + HITL plugin escalate to human agents; Start HITL Card placed in a Workflow triggers the handoff and the bot resumes if the user cancels or queue times out.

evidence.title: Botpress llms.txt — Desk HITL
evidence.quote: Escalate conversations to support agents in Botpress Desk.

evidence.title: Botpress HITL plugin
evidence.quote: The Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) integration allows members of your Workspace to ... Add the Start HITL Card at the point where you want to hand off conversations to a live agent

members.note: Conversation variables persist within one conversation and are cleared at end; ADK 'Manage state' exposes user-scoped and bot-scoped state, but the documented default scope is the single conversation.

evidence.title: Botpress conversation variables
evidence.quote: Information stored in conversation variables is deleted when the current conversation is over.

evidence.title: Botpress ADK — manage state
evidence.quote: Store and reuse data across conversations, users, and the entire bot.

members.note: Triggers are first-class nodes that only execute when a named event (Conversation Started, Custom Trigger from Webchat, integration event) fires.

evidence.title: Botpress triggers
evidence.quote: Triggers are a special kind of Node that only execute when a certain event occurs.

evidence.title: Botpress triggers — custom
evidence.quote: You can use Custom Triggers to make Webchat react to custom events from your website.

members.note: Botpress ships a Translator Agent in its 8-agent pipeline; voice-specific multilingual handling is not a documented headline feature.

evidence.title: Botpress llms.txt — translator agent
evidence.quote: Enables your bot to communicate in multiple languages

evidence.title: Botpress agents introduction — Translator role
evidence.quote: Translator Agent: Enables multi-language communication

members.note: Conversation-scoped variables auto-isolate per conversation and are deleted at end; bot/user/conversation scopes are explicit.

evidence.title: Botpress conversation variables — scope
evidence.quote: Conversation variables let you store and reuse information within the scope of a single conversation.

evidence.title: Botpress conversation variables — cleanup
evidence.quote: Information stored in conversation variables is deleted when the current conversation is over.

members.note: Autonomous Node calls user-defined tools; ADK 'Define tools' API exposes them to the LLM.

evidence.title: Botpress Autonomous Node — tools
evidence.quote: Unlike a standard Node, which executes its Cards one by one, the Autonomous Node uses a Large Language Model to decide when to execute tools.

evidence.title: Botpress ADK — define tools
evidence.quote: Give the AI model functions it can call during execution.

members.note: Studio is a visual, drag-and-drop interface for building agents; Workflows are graphs of Nodes connected by transitions.

evidence.title: Botpress Studio description
evidence.quote: Visual, drag-and-drop interface for building AI agents

evidence.title: Botpress Autonomous Node — transitions
evidence.quote: Transitions to a sub-Workflow ... Transitions to a Node that's connected to the Autonomous Node.

members.note: Honest downgrade — the docs index calls out Workflows as 'long-running background processes that operate independently of conversations' but no cron-style scheduled-agent primitive surfaced on pages reviewed.

evidence.title: Botpress llms.txt — workflows
evidence.quote: Documentation for building long-running background processes that operate independently of conversations.

members.note: Approval gating is not a documented first-class primitive; the HITL flow includes a 'Queue Timeout' for waiting on a human agent rather than gating tool calls.

evidence.title: Botpress HITL Agent — Queue Timeout
evidence.quote: This is the maximum wait time the bot will wait for an agent to handle the user's query.

members.note: Honest downgrade — the v12 OSS engine supported custom JS Actions and the current ADK is a TypeScript library, but no documented sandbox for LLM-emitted code execution surfaced on the pages fetched.

evidence.title: Botpress v12 README — TypeScript SDK
evidence.quote: Developer Tools: CLI, SDK (@botpress/sdk), and API client (@botpress/client)

references.title: botpress/botpress repo
references.quote: the ultimate platform for building next-generation chatbots and assistants powered by OpenAI

compositions.name: Microsoft Bot Framework
compositions.intent: Microsoft's pre-LLM SDK for building turn-based conversational bots in C#/JS/Python/Java with dialog stacks, channel adapters, scoped state, and a documented handoff-to-human protocol; archived on GitHub with support ending December 2025.
compositions.description: Microsoft Bot Framework SDK v4 is a modular SDK and set of services (Azure Bot Service, Bot Connector) for building bots that converse across channels (Teams, Slack, Telegram, Facebook, web chat, SMS via Twilio, and others). Bots are activity-driven (each user message is a turn), use the Dialog library (component, waterfall, prompt, adaptive dialogs) to manage multi-turn flows, persist state in three scopes (user / conversation / private-conversation) over Memory / Azure Blob / Cosmos storage, and support a standardised handoff protocol with `handoff.initiate` and `handoff.status` events. The SDK and Bot Framework Emulator have been archived; Microsoft recommends migrating to the Microsoft 365 Agents SDK.
primary_use_cases: multi-channel enterprise chatbots (Teams, Slack, Facebook Messenger, web chat, SMS)
dialog-stack-driven multi-turn conversation flows with waterfall and prompts
QnA Maker / Custom Question Answering knowledge bots
bot-as-proxy or bot-as-agent handoff to human-agent platforms
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Activity-driven turn loop: the Bot Connector relays an activity from a channel to the bot, the bot's turn handler reads/writes state through state property accessors, the active Dialog on the dialog stack continues from where it left off (multi-turn persistence via dialog state), the bot replies, and state is saved. There is no LLM agent loop in the SDK itself — the bot is imperative; LUIS/QnA Maker provide NLU.

key_concepts.name: Activity / Turn
key_concepts.summary: A bot processes activities one turn at a time; conversation state must be re-read each turn.

key_concepts.name: Dialogs
key_concepts.summary: Container, component, waterfall, prompt, adaptive, skill, and QnA Maker dialogs manage multi-turn flows via a dialog stack.

key_concepts.name: State buckets (user / conversation / private-conversation)
key_concepts.summary: Three scoped state buckets persisted via state property accessors over Memory / Blob / Cosmos.

key_concepts.name: Channels / Bot Connector
key_concepts.summary: Connect a single bot to Teams, Slack, Facebook, Telegram, SMS, web chat with a normalised activity stream.

key_concepts.name: Handoff protocol
key_concepts.summary: Standard `handoff.initiate` and `handoff.status` events for bot-to-human escalation; bot-as-agent and bot-as-proxy integration models.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User on channel] --> conn[Bot Connector]
  conn --> turn[Turn handler]
  turn --> state[Read state buckets: user/conversation/private]
  state --> stack[Active Dialog from dialog stack]
  stack --> dlg{Dialog type}
  dlg -->|Waterfall| step[Sequential step]
  dlg -->|Prompt| ask[Ask user]
  dlg -->|QnA Maker| kb[Knowledge base]
  dlg -->|Skill| skill[Delegate to skill bot]
  step --> reply[Send activity]
  ask --> reply
  kb --> reply
  skill --> reply
  reply --> handoff{Escalate?}
  handoff -->|yes| ev[handoff.initiate event]
  ev --> hub[Agent hub]
  hub --> stat[handoff.status: accepted / failed / completed]
  handoff -->|no| save[Save state]
  stat --> save

members.note: Standardised handoff protocol with `handoff.initiate` and `handoff.status` events, plus two named integration models (bot-as-agent and bot-as-proxy) and a Handoff Library sample.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — handoff initiation event
evidence.quote: When a bot detects the need to hand the conversation off to an agent, it signals its intent by sending a handoff initiation event.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — handoff status event
evidence.quote: A handoff status event is sent to the bot by the agent hub. The event informs the bot about the status of the initiated handoff operation.

members.note: Three predefined scoped state buckets (user / conversation / private-conversation), each keyed by channel-id plus user/conversation id.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — state buckets
evidence.quote: The SDK includes three of these 'buckets': User state, Conversation state, Private conversation state

evidence.title: Bot Framework — keying
evidence.quote: Both user and conversation state are scoped by channel. The same person using different channels to access your bot appears as different users

members.note: Adaptive Dialogs and the Dispatch tool route between LUIS / QnA Maker recognisers, but no headline 'fallback-chain' primitive is named in the v4 docs; closest first-class concept is the QnA Maker dialog's follow-up prompts.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — QnA Maker follow-up prompts
evidence.quote: Follow-up prompts, also known as multi-turn prompts, allow a knowledge base to ask the user for more information before answering their question.

members.note: Bots can call APIs and external services from turn-handler code, but the SDK predates LLM tool-calling — no native tool-call primitive.

evidence.title: Bot Framework overview — bots as web apps
evidence.quote: Bots can do the same things other types of software can do—read from and write to files, use databases and APIs

members.note: Dialog stack is the SDK's explicit multi-turn plan; component dialogs encapsulate sub-plans, waterfall dialogs sequence steps, prompt dialogs gather inputs.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — dialog stack
evidence.quote: A dialog context contains information about all active dialogs and includes a dialog stack, which acts as a call stack for all the active dialogs.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — waterfall dialog
evidence.quote: waterfall dialog | Defines a sequence of steps, allowing your bot to guide a user through a linear process.

members.note: User state is keyed by channel-id+from-id and persists across conversations; can be backed by Azure Blob or Cosmos partitioned storage.

evidence.title: Bot Framework — user state
evidence.quote: User state is available in any turn that the bot is conversing with that user on that channel, regardless of the conversation

evidence.title: Bot Framework — storage options
evidence.quote: Azure Blob Storage connects to an Azure Blob Storage object database. Azure Cosmos DB partitioned storage connects to a partitioned Cosmos DB NoSQL database.

references.title: What is the Bot Framework SDK?
references.quote: Microsoft Bot Framework and Azure AI Bot Service are a collection of libraries, tools, and services that let you build, test, deploy, and manage intelligent bots.

references.title: microsoft/botframework-sdk
references.quote: Bot Framework provides the most comprehensive experience for building conversation applications.

references.title: Bot Framework SDK retirement notice
references.quote: The Bot Framework SDK and Bot Framework Emulator have been archived on GitHub. The project is no longer updated or maintained. Support tickets for the Bot Framework SDK will no longer be serviced as of December 31, 2025.

compositions.name: Rasa
compositions.intent: Developer platform for enterprise text and voice AI assistants combining LLM-based dialogue understanding (CALM) with deterministic business logic encoded as Flows, custom actions for tool calls, slots for collected data, and built-in patterns for human handoff and fallback.
compositions.description: Rasa is an open-source (legacy Open Source) and commercial (Rasa Platform / Pro) framework for building text and voice conversational assistants. The current dialogue engine is CALM (Conversational AI with Language Models): an LLM interprets each user message and emits internal commands (start flow, set slot, correct slot, cancel flow, clarify flows, chitchat, knowledge answer, human handoff), while a deterministic dialogue manager executes those commands against business logic written as Flows. Flows are step-by-step processes built from collect (slot filling), set_slots, action (custom-action server call), and branching steps; the tracker persists slot values across turns.
primary_use_cases: enterprise customer-support and CX assistants over text and voice
LLM-driven dialogue understanding with deterministic flows enforcing business logic
custom-action-driven tool use against internal APIs
human handoff via the built-in human_handoff command
knowledge-grounded answers via Enterprise RAG and the knowledge_answer command
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Each turn the user message is fed to a CALM dialogue-understanding LLM with a structured prompt that emits a set of internal commands (start flow, set slot, correct slot, cancel flow, clarify flows, chitchat, knowledge answer, human handoff). The dialogue manager interprets those commands against the currently active Flow on the tracker, executing collect / set_slots / action / branching steps. Custom actions run in an external action server; the tracker carries slot state across turns. Conversation patterns provide reusable structured sub-flows for things like clarification, correction, and handoff.

key_concepts.name: CALM
key_concepts.summary: Conversational AI with Language Models — the dialogue system pairing an LLM-driven dialogue understanding layer with a deterministic dialogue manager.

key_concepts.name: Flows
key_concepts.summary: Step-by-step descriptions of business processes (collect slots, run actions, branch on data); the LLM can dynamically route between flows.

key_concepts.name: Dialogue understanding / commands
key_concepts.summary: LLM emits internal commands (start flow, set slot, correct slot, cancel flow, clarify flows, chitchat, knowledge answer, human handoff) for the dialogue manager to execute.

key_concepts.name: Slots & tracker
key_concepts.summary: Slots are typed values collected through flow steps; the tracker is per-conversation persistent state.

key_concepts.name: Custom actions
key_concepts.summary: External action server endpoints called from action steps for tool/API integration.

key_concepts.name: Human handoff
key_concepts.summary: Built-in command and conversation pattern for escalating to a live agent.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User message] --> du[Dialogue understanding LLM]
  du --> cmds[Commands: start flow / set slot / cancel / chitchat / knowledge answer / human handoff]
  cmds --> dm[Dialogue manager]
  dm --> flow{Active Flow step}
  flow -->|collect| ask[Ask user, set slot]
  flow -->|set_slots| upd[Update tracker]
  flow -->|action| act[Custom action server call]
  flow -->|branch| br[Branch on slot value]
  ask --> tracker[Tracker slots]
  upd --> tracker
  act --> tracker
  br --> tracker
  tracker --> respond[Bot response]
  cmds -->|human_handoff| handoff[Live-agent handoff pattern]
  cmds -->|knowledge_answer| rag[Enterprise RAG]
  rag --> respond
  handoff --> respond

members.note: Enterprise RAG is a named Rasa product; the `knowledge_answer` command is a built-in CALM command that routes to grounded answers.

evidence.title: Rasa Enterprise RAG
evidence.quote: Retrieve information in real time, so every answer is fresh, verifiable, and aligned.

evidence.title: Rasa dialogue understanding — commands
evidence.quote: start flow, cancel flow, clarify flows, set slot, correct slot, chitchat, knowledge answer, human handoff

members.note: `human handoff` is one of the documented built-in commands the dialogue understanding layer emits.

evidence.title: Rasa dialogue understanding — human handoff command
evidence.quote: start flow, cancel flow, clarify flows, set slot, correct slot, chitchat, knowledge answer, human handoff

members.note: Rasa positions itself explicitly around 'built-in recovery patterns'; clarify_flows, correct_slot, cancel_flow act as the LLM-side fallback chain.

evidence.title: Rasa platform positioning
evidence.quote: Rasa extends LLMs with structured flows, deterministic logic, and built-in recovery patterns.

evidence.title: Rasa dialogue understanding — recovery commands
evidence.quote: These next steps are communicated as a set of 'commands' for the dialogue manager to follow and might include instructions to continue a flow, start a new flow, or activate conversation pattern flows.

members.note: Slots set inside a flow are reset when the flow ends — documented per-flow isolation.

evidence.title: Rasa flows — slot lifecycle
evidence.quote: By default, slots set in a `collect` and `set_slot` step are reset when the flow ends.

members.note: CALM's whole architecture is built on the LLM emitting a constrained, typed command set rather than free text — the canonical structured-output use.

evidence.title: Rasa CALM — LLM constrained to commands
evidence.quote: CALM assistants use an LLM for dialogue understanding within a controlled framework. A structured prompt ensures reliable and predictable interpretation.

evidence.title: Rasa CALM — separation of concerns
evidence.quote: In CALM assistants, LLMs keep the conversation fluent but don't guess your business logic.

members.note: Action steps in flows trigger custom actions served by an external action server, used for API and tool calls.

evidence.title: Rasa flows — action steps
evidence.quote: It describes the information you need from the user, data you need to retrieve from an API or a database, and branching logic based on the information collected.

evidence.title: Rasa flows — step definition
evidence.quote: Each flow describes the logical steps your AI assistant uses to complete a task.

members.note: Flows are predefined plans; the LLM picks which flow to start and the dialogue manager executes it deterministically.

evidence.title: Rasa CALM — flows are business plans
evidence.quote: Business logic, step-by-step outlines of how to solve a problem, is defined in Flows. The LLM can dynamically route from flow to flow.

evidence.title: Rasa CALM — structured workflows
evidence.quote: Follows structured workflows for reliable, debuggable interactions.

references.title: RasaHQ/rasa
references.quote: An open source machine learning framework for automating text and voice-based conversations.

references.title: Rasa platform overview
references.quote: Rasa extends LLMs with structured flows, deterministic logic, and built-in recovery patterns.

references.title: Rasa CALM
references.quote: CALM (Conversational AI with Language Models) is the dialogue system that runs Rasa text and voice assistants.

compositions.name: Voiceflow
compositions.intent: SaaS visual platform for designing AI customer-experience agents that mix agentic playbooks (LLM-driven goal-based reasoning with tools) and deterministic workflows (visual step graphs), deployed across chat, voice, and call-centre channels.
compositions.description: Voiceflow is a hosted platform for building AI agents for support, lead generation, and other customer experiences. Builders mix two primitives: Playbooks (goal-based LLM agents with tools and instructions that decide how to reach an outcome) and Workflows (visual, deterministic step graphs with branches, conditions, and integrations). Agents have a global prompt, instructions, a knowledge base for RAG, and global tools available everywhere. The Crew step coordinates multiple playbooks with direct handoffs between them. Tools include API tools, MCP tools, Function tools (custom JS), system tools (knowledge base, call forwarding, web search, UI artefacts), and pre-built integrations. Deployment surfaces include chat and voice widgets and call-centre IVR.
primary_use_cases: AI customer support agents with playbook + workflow design
lead generation and qualification conversational agents
voice / IVR agents for call-centre automation
knowledge-base RAG agents grounded in uploaded documents and Zendesk/Shopify connectors
multi-playbook crews coordinating sales, billing, and support sub-agents
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Hybrid agentic/deterministic loop. The agent has a global prompt + instructions and may be in an agentic context (top-level agent, playbook, or crew step — LLM reasons freely with global tools) or a scripted context (workflow step — deterministic, global tools disabled). Playbooks are goal-based: the LLM decides which tool to call (API tool, MCP tool, function tool, integration, system tool) toward the configured outcome. The Crew step nests multiple playbooks that hand control between each other based on each playbook's LLM description. Workflows can embed playbooks via the Playbook step for agentic-inside-scripted hybrids.

key_concepts.name: Playbooks
key_concepts.summary: Goal-based agentic primitives — LLM decides how to reach the configured outcome with tools.

key_concepts.name: Workflows
key_concepts.summary: Visual, deterministic multi-step builder with branches, conditions, integrations.

key_concepts.name: Crew step
key_concepts.summary: Coordinates multiple playbooks that hand off tasks to each other within one workflow step.

key_concepts.name: Knowledge base
key_concepts.summary: Upload URLs/sitemaps/documents or connect Zendesk/Shopify/Kustomer/Salesforce for RAG grounding.

key_concepts.name: Tools (API / MCP / Function / Integration / System / Global)
key_concepts.summary: Six tool kinds the agent can call; Global tools are agent-level and available in any agentic context.

key_concepts.name: Global prompt / instructions
key_concepts.summary: Per-turn agent prompt (personality/goal/tone/guardrails) plus routing instructions for skill selection.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User on channel] --> gp[Global prompt + instructions]
  gp --> route{Agent decides}
  route -->|agentic| pb[Playbook]
  route -->|scripted| wf[Workflow]
  pb --> tools[Tools: API / MCP / Function / Integration / System / Global]
  wf --> step{Step type}
  step -->|Playbook step| pb
  step -->|Crew step| crew[Crew of playbooks]
  step -->|Scripted| det[Buttons / listen / message]
  crew --> pb2[Other playbook in crew]
  pb2 --> tools
  tools --> kb[Knowledge base RAG]
  kb --> reply[Reply on chat/voice/IVR]
  det --> reply
  pb --> reply

members.note: Knowledge base ingests URLs, sitemaps, documents, and external sources (Zendesk, Shopify, Kustomer, Salesforce) for RAG grounding; explicitly called out in the docs as 'ground responses in real information via RAG'.

evidence.title: Voiceflow build overview — knowledge base via RAG
evidence.quote: Upload URLs, sitemaps, and documents to ground responses in real information via RAG.

evidence.title: Voiceflow knowledge base
evidence.quote: Add knowledge to your agent & connect to tools like Zendesk & Shopify.

members.note: Crew step lets playbooks hand off tasks between each other directly; system tools include 'forward calls' for voice channels.

evidence.title: Voiceflow Crew step
evidence.quote: The Crew step lets you group multiple playbooks together so they can hand off tasks between each other directly.

evidence.title: Voiceflow playbook tools — system tools list
evidence.quote: System tools —  call the knowledge base, forward calls, end the conversation, display buttons, cards or carousels, search the web.

members.note: Documented as a native capability across voice and chat channels.

evidence.title: Voiceflow docs intro — multilingual
evidence.quote: Resolve customer issues through natural conversation in any language

evidence.title: Voiceflow channels — voice / call centre
evidence.quote: Bring conversational AI to your call centres for automated, human-level call handling

members.note: Six documented tool kinds (API, MCP, Function, Integration, System, Global) callable from playbooks; tool description plus LLM signal drive selection.

evidence.title: Voiceflow playbooks — tools list
evidence.quote: Integrations ... API tool ... MCP tool ... Function tool ... System tools

evidence.title: Voiceflow global tools
evidence.quote: Global tools are APIs, integrations, MCPs, and functions that you add directly at the agent level. Once added, they're available at the agent level, and in any playbook throughout your AI agent

members.note: Workflows are explicitly a visual builder; playbooks and workflows are the two named primitives of the build surface.

evidence.title: Voiceflow build overview — workflows
evidence.quote: Workflows: Visual workflow builder for more deterministic, multi-step conversation flows with branching, conditions, and integrations.

evidence.title: Voiceflow docs intro — design agents visually
evidence.quote: Design agents visually with playbooks, workflows, and a knowledge base.

members.note: Playbook = goal-based plan; workflow = explicit scripted execution; the two are mixed by embedding a Playbook step inside a Workflow.

evidence.title: Voiceflow playbooks — goal-based
evidence.quote: Playbooks are goal-based. You define an outcome ... and the agent navigates the conversation to get there.

evidence.title: Voiceflow build overview — hybrid
evidence.quote: Workflows can use playbooks & other AI-powered steps, so you can build agentic experiences with deterministic control where it matters.

members.note: Crew step nests a root playbook with child playbooks that hand control between each other and back to the root.

evidence.title: Voiceflow Crew step — root + children
evidence.quote: The root playbook is simply the first playbook triggered when the workflow enters the Crew step. There is no functional difference between the root playbook and other playbooks beyond this.

evidence.title: Voiceflow Crew step — mutual awareness
evidence.quote: Each playbook in the crew is aware of the strengths of other playbooks and can hand off tasks to them.

members.note: Instructions define routing across skills; each playbook's LLM description tells the top-level agent or crew when to route into it.

evidence.title: Voiceflow build overview — instructions for routing
evidence.quote: Instructions: Defines how the agent routes requests and decides which skills to use.

evidence.title: Voiceflow playbooks — LLM description for routing
evidence.quote: The description should cover both what the playbook does and when the agent should route into it — this is what other playbooks, workflows, and the top-level agent read when deciding whether to call it.

references.title: Voiceflow homepage
references.quote: Build, launch, and scale advanced AI agents for support, lead generation, and beyond

references.title: Voiceflow build overview
references.quote: Build AI agents with playbooks, workflows, and powerful customization options.
